statement.markdown: Implement an efficient function to check if a given string is a palindrome. The function should return true if the string is a palindrome and false otherwise. Consider a string as a palindrome if it reads the same from left to right and from right to left, ignoring spaces and punctuation. The function should also be case-insensitive.
statement.inputFormat: A string containing characters, spaces, and punctuation.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean indicating whether the string is a palindrome.
statement.notes: The function should be efficient, i.e., it should not use any extra space that scales with the input size. It should also handle edge cases such as empty strings, strings with only one character, and strings with multiple spaces or punctuation.

examples.input: A man, a plan, a canal: Panama
examples.explanation: The string is a palindrome when ignoring spaces and punctuation.
The string is not a palindrome.

hints.text: Use a two-pointer approach to check the string from both ends.

editorial.title: Efficient Palindrome Checker
editorial.intuition: A palindrome is a string that reads the same forwards and backwards. To check if a string is a palindrome, we can use a two-pointer approach, starting from both ends of the string and moving towards the center.
editorial.bruteForce: A naive approach would be to reverse the string and compare it with the original string. However, this approach has a high time complexity of O(n), where n is the length of the string.
editorial.betterSolution: The two-pointer approach has a time complexity of O(n), but it uses a constant amount of extra space, making it more efficient than the naive approach.
editorial.optimalSolution: The two-pointer approach is the optimal solution for this problem.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The two-pointer approach correctly checks if a string is a palindrome by comparing characters from both ends.
editorial.dryRun: Example run: Input string 'A man, a plan, a canal: Panama'. Output: True.

starterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: bool solve(string s); };
starterCode.java: class Solution { public boolean solve(String s); }
starterCode.python: class Solution: def solve(self, s: str) -> bool:

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: bool solve(string s) { int i = 0, j = s.length() - 1; while (i < j) { if (s[i] != s[j]) return false; i++; } return true; } };
buggyStarterCode.java: class Solution { public boolean solve(String s) { int i = 0, j = s.length() - 1; while (i < j) { if (s.charAt(i) != s.charAt(j)) return false; i++; } return true; } }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def solve(self, s: str) -> bool: i, j = 0, len(s) - 1 while i < j: if s[i] != s[j]: return False i += 1 return True
buggyStarterCode.javascript: function solve(s) { var i = 0, j = s.length - 1; while (i < j) { if (s[i] != s[j]) return false; i++; } return true; }

solutions.cpp: class Solution { public: bool solve(string s) { int i = 0, j = s.length() - 1; while (i < j) { if (s[i] != s[j]) return false; i++; } return true; } };
solutions.java: class Solution { public boolean solve(String s) { int i = 0, j = s.length() - 1; while (i < j) { if (s.charAt(i) != s.charAt(j)) return false; i++; } return true; } }
